metadata.title: Cosmic Horror Index — NLP-Grounded Dataset
metadata.methodology: Automated scoring via semantic retrieval + LLM classification of sacred text corpora

axes.label: Omniscience
axes.description: Does the entity or ultimate reality know everything?
probes_high: the divine knows all things past present and future
nothing is hidden from the supreme being
the ultimate reality is all-knowing and all-seeing
the cosmic consciousness perceives every thought and action
infinite knowledge and awareness beyond human comprehension
Odin gave his eye at Mimirs well to gain all wisdom and see all hidden things
probes_low: the gods have limited knowledge
the divine can be deceived or surprised
there is no all-knowing entity
knowledge is distributed among many beings

axes.label: Omnipotence
probes_high: the supreme being has unlimited power over all creation
nothing can resist the will of the divine
the ultimate reality creates and destroys at will
the cosmic force is irresistible and absolute
all things are possible for the highest power
the gods possess power over creation giants and men must yield to divine will
probes_low: the gods have limited power and can be challenged
divine power is constrained by rules or other forces
no single entity has absolute power

axes.label: Self-sufficiency
axes.description: Does the entity need anything from creation?
probes_high: the divine needs nothing from creation or creatures
the supreme being is complete and lacking nothing
worship and praise are unnecessary to the absolute
the ultimate reality is self-existent and self-sustaining
the cosmos operates independently of human participation
probes_low: the gods require offerings and sacrifices
divine power depends on human worship or action
the cosmic order requires human participation to maintain

axes.label: Indifference to humans
axes.description: Does the entity care about human welfare?
probes_high: the ultimate reality is indifferent to human suffering
the cosmos operates without concern for human welfare
divine power does not respond to prayer or supplication
human existence is irrelevant to the cosmic order
the supreme being neither loves nor hates creation
the universe continues regardless of human action
the kami are capricious and do not respond to human prayer with consistent care
probes_low: the divine loves and cares for humanity
the supreme being responds to prayer and devotion
the cosmic order is designed for human benefit
the gods are moved by human suffering

axes.label: Incomprehensibility
axes.description: Can humans understand the ultimate reality?
probes_high: the divine nature is beyond human understanding
the ultimate reality cannot be described in words
human minds cannot grasp the infinite
attempting to know the absolute leads to madness or confusion
the supreme being is ineffable and unknowable
language and thought fail when approaching the divine
the runes hold mysteries beyond mortal understanding their power is vast and unknowable
the kami are mysterious forces musubi creative power beyond human comprehension
probes_low: the divine can be known through scripture or revelation
human reason can understand the nature of reality
the gods communicate clearly with humanity

axes.label: Human insignificance
axes.description: Do humans matter in this cosmological system?
probes_high: humanity is insignificant in the cosmic scale
human civilization is a momentary phenomenon
individual human life has no ultimate meaning or purpose
the universe existed for vast ages before humans and will continue after
human concerns are trivial compared to cosmic forces
men are fated to die at Ragnarok the Norns weave the destiny of all beings
the lives of men are brief and meaningless against the vast ages of the gods
probes_low: humanity has a special role in creation
the cosmos was made for human benefit
human actions have cosmic significance
the divine values each human soul

axes.label: Cyclical destruction
axes.description: Does the cosmos destroy itself and rebuild?
probes_high: the universe is destroyed and recreated in endless cycles
worlds end in flood fire or catastrophe and are remade
the cosmic order dissolves back into chaos periodically
creation and destruction alternate eternally
the end of the world is a recurring event not a singular one
Ragnarok the doom of the gods when the world sinks into the sea and rises anew
the world shall be destroyed in fire and flood and a new earth rise from the waters
probes_low: creation happened once and is permanent
the cosmos has a single linear history
the world will end once and be replaced by something eternal

axes.label: Awe / madness induction
axes.description: Does encountering the truth break human minds?
probes_high: encountering the divine causes terror and trembling
seeing the true form of reality overwhelms the mind
knowledge of the ultimate truth drives humans to madness
the direct experience of the absolute is unbearable
mortals cannot look upon the divine and survive unchanged
Odin hung nine nights on Yggdrasil the world tree wounded by a spear to gain the runes
the vision of Ragnarok fills the seeress with terror and dread
ritual purification misogi is required after contact with the powerful and dangerous sacred
the kami manifest with overwhelming power that humans cannot endure directly
probes_low: the divine is approachable and comforting
knowledge of truth brings peace and joy
encountering the divine is a positive experience

axes.label: Creation without consent
axes.description: Were humans made without being asked?
probes_high: humanity was created by divine will without being consulted
existence was imposed upon creatures by the creator
beings are born into a cosmos they did not choose
the world arose from impersonal forces without intent
life emerges from unconscious processes
Izanagi and Izanami created the islands and kami from the primordial void without asking
probes_low: souls chose to incarnate
beings participate in their own creation
creation is a collaborative act between divine and human

axes.label: Moral neutrality
axes.description: Is the ultimate reality beyond good and evil?
probes_high: the cosmos is neither good nor evil
the ultimate reality transcends moral categories
natural forces operate without moral judgment
the divine encompasses both creation and destruction equally
good and evil are human constructions not cosmic truths
the gods fight the giants not from virtue but from the necessity of fate
impurity kegare is not moral evil but a natural state requiring ritual cleansing
probes_low: the divine is essentially good and opposed to evil
the cosmos has a moral order
good will ultimately triumph over evil
the supreme being judges between right and wrong

evidence.justification: The passage explicitly states that 'Past, present, future, all are one in Yog-Sothoth' and describes comprehensive knowledge of the Old Ones' movements across time and space, indicating near-total omniscience.

The passage suggests limited, gradually revealed knowledge ('Much was told me last night... more will be subsequently communicated') rather than complete omniscience, indicating knowledge transmission rather than total awareness.
The passage suggests the Outer Ones possess vast knowledge spanning cosmic scales and can manipulate human discovery, implying superior but not explicitly total omniscience.
The passage suggests limited knowledge among cosmic entities - even Great Cthulhu can only 'spy Them dimly' - indicating imperfect awareness rather than omniscience.

The passage mentions Zadok having a 'knowing leer' and giving 'hints that could not be mistaken,' suggesting some form of knowledge or awareness, but this appears to be limited human knowledge rather than omniscient divine awareness.

The passage mentions 'the earth mutters with Their consciousness' suggesting some form of awareness, but focuses more on the entities' hiddenness and unknowability rather than their knowledge of all things.

The passage describes beings whose domain encompasses reality as we know it as merely 'an atom in the genuine infinity which is theirs,' strongly suggesting vast, reality-transcending power that approaches unlimited scope.

The passage describes sea-gods that can deliver tangible results (fish, gold) unlike the ineffective Christian heaven, suggesting entities with real power to grant material benefits, though the extent of their omnipotence isn't fully specified.

The passage describes an overwhelming, unnatural cataclysm that completely transforms reality and sends fragments that 'our universe must needs disown,' suggesting immense power that operates beyond natural constraints, though it focuses more on the manifestation of power than explicit omnipotence.

The passage describes an entity that 'obeyed laws that are not of our cosmos' and comes from 'unformed realms of infinity beyond all Nature,' suggesting power that transcends normal physical constraints, though it doesn't explicitly address unlimited power or ability to do anything.

The passage describes beings who have lived since before humanity and possess immortality, suggesting power that transcends normal limitations, but doesn't directly address unlimited creative/destructive power or absolute omnipotence.

The passage describes an alien substance with properties that defy earthly understanding and obey 'outside laws,' suggesting power beyond normal constraints, but this refers to material properties rather than a conscious entity's omnipotence.

The passage suggests the Outer Ones have limitations - they need human cooperation, require secrecy for their outposts, and must adapt to expanding human knowledge, indicating their power is not unlimited and can be challenged by circumstances.

The passage describes an entity that can move instantaneously through the sky and disappear through clouds without leaving a trail, suggesting supernatural power, but the scope of abilities shown is limited to movement rather than unlimited creative/destructive force.

The passage suggests an entity that operates 'against Nature' and comes from 'some place whar things ain't as they is here,' implying power that transcends natural laws, but doesn't explicitly describe unlimited power or the ability to do anything.

The passage describes alien entities but focuses on their disturbing appearance rather than their power level, and suggests they are numerous rather than omnipotent, implying distributed rather than unlimited divine power.

The passage explicitly describes a transactional relationship where the sea-things require human sacrifices and carved offerings from humans in exchange for fish and gold, demonstrating clear dependence on creation for sustenance.

The passage explicitly describes a transactional relationship where the cosmic entities require 'sacrifices' and 'savage knick-knacks' from humans, indicating clear dependence on offerings from creation rather than self-sufficiency.

The passage explicitly describes gods that require sacrifices from humans and provide benefits (good fishing) in return, indicating divine entities that need something from creation rather than being self-sufficient.

The passage describes gods that 'give somethin' ye reely need' in exchange for worship and service, contrasting with the Christian heaven that 'didn't help 'em none,' indicating these entities require human devotion and action to provide benefits.

The passage describes cosmic entities that exist independently across all time and dimensions, walking 'serene and primal' without any indication they require human worship, offerings, or actions to sustain themselves or their cosmic functions.

The passage describes entities that come to Earth specifically to obtain metals from mines, indicating they have material needs from creation rather than being self-sufficient, which supports a high score on the self-sufficiency axis.

The passage describes cosmic entities whose main existence is in 'strangely organised abysses wholly beyond the utmost reach of any human imagination' and for whom our entire universe is merely 'an atom in the genuine infinity which is theirs,' strongly suggesting beings that are utterly self-sufficient and need nothing from our reality.

The passage suggests the Outer Ones have needs from humanity - they 'desire to know mankind more fully' and require human interpreters, indicating some dependence on creation rather than complete self-sufficiency.

The passage describes a cosmic entity or force that operates independently without any indication of requiring human worship, offerings, or acknowledgment, suggesting self-sufficiency, though the focus is more on the entity's destructive departure than its needs.

The passage describes an alien substance that operates by 'outside laws' independent of earthly understanding, suggesting a cosmic entity that functions autonomously without needing anything from terrestrial creation.

The passage depicts cosmic entities ('They from outside') that view humans merely as tools for their own purposes, showing complete indifference to human welfare as evidenced by casual violence against humans and plans to 'clear off the earth.'

The passage describes non-human entities that treat humans merely as sources of information and operate entirely for their own purposes (mining metals), showing clear indifference to human welfare or significance in cosmic operations.

The passage describes Outer Beings as cosmic entities composed of alien matter with fundamentally different properties from earthly life, positioning humans implicitly as merely 'degenerate variants' of a superior cosmos-wide race, suggesting profound indifference to human-specific concerns.

The passage depicts cosmic entities whose vast scale and operations ('strangely organised abysses wholly beyond the utmost reach of any human imagination') render human concerns insignificant, with humans existing as mere atoms within their 'genuine infinity.'

The passage depicts cosmic entities that are indifferent to human welfare, viewing humans as irrelevant unless they become inconvenient, and would 'rather leave things as they are to save bother' rather than engage with humanity meaningfully.

The passage depicts cosmic horrors that exist independent of human comprehension or welfare, suggesting an indifferent universe where humans encounter realities that shatter their mental peace and understanding without any divine concern for their suffering.

The passage describes an alien entity from 'unformed realms of infinity beyond all Nature' that operates according to non-terrestrial laws, strongly implying cosmic indifference to human understanding or welfare through its complete alienness and the terror it induces.
The passage depicts cosmic forces that operate with complete disregard for human presence or welfare, creating destruction and horror without any consideration for human consequences or suffering.

The passage depicts gods that provide material benefits (fish and gold) in exchange for service, rather than caring about human welfare or moral flourishing, suggesting a transactional rather than loving relationship with humans.

The passage describes ancient cosmic beings that withdrew before advancing humanity, suggesting these powers exist independently of and are indifferent to human concerns, though it doesn't explicitly address care or welfare.

The passage explicitly describes cosmic entities as 'undimensioned and to us unseen' and states 'no one can behold Them,' indicating these ultimate realities are beyond human perception and understanding, though it doesn't completely deny the possibility of knowledge since the text itself conveys information about them.

The passage describes knowledge/experience that cannot be communicated without appearing insane, involves 'unnamable abysses' that resist comprehension, and causes mental deterioration when encountered - strongly indicating reality beyond human understanding.

The passage explicitly describes 'remote secrets and unimaginable abysses' and 'unknown and inhuman evil' that overwhelms human understanding, with the narrator experiencing unease and discomfort when confronted with these incomprehensible designs that hint at realities beyond human grasp.

The passage directly questions the limits of human understanding and the inability to distinguish between reality and delusion when confronted with incomprehensible forces, suggesting that ultimate reality may be beyond human grasp.

The passage describes phenomena that defy scientific understanding - samples that produce 'unknown spectrum' and 'baffling bands' that cannot be comprehended through normal analytical methods, suggesting reality beyond human comprehension.

The passage depicts the protagonist being drawn to 'stupendous and unheard-of splendours' that await in cosmic depths, suggesting realities beyond normal human comprehension, while his transformation involves experiencing things that would typically overwhelm human understanding.

The passage depicts something that defies natural understanding and scientific reasoning ('It wasn't right—it was against Nature'), originating from a realm where fundamental reality operates differently, suggesting limits to human comprehension of ultimate reality.
The passage suggests that fully comprehending the cosmic horror would drive one to total madness, indicating reality is beyond human understanding and dangerous to contemplate.

The passage describes Nyarlathotep as operating through deception ('semblance of men,' 'waxen mask,' 'robe that hides') and coming from incomprehensible cosmic realms ('world of Seven Suns,' 'the Gulf') to 'mock' humanity, suggesting the divine reality is fundamentally alien and beyond human comprehension.

The passage describes the protagonist's inability to fully grasp or articulate 'something hideous' and questions whether what's approaching is madness or something beyond normal comprehension, suggesting reality that exceeds human understanding.

The passage explicitly states that our entire cosmic reality is merely 'an atom in the genuine infinity' of the Outer Ones, directly depicting humans and their entire universe as cosmically insignificant compared to vastly superior beings.

The passage describes Outer Beings as 'the most marvellous organic things in or beyond all space and time' with humans and 'all other life-forms' being merely 'degenerate variants' of this superior cosmic race, strongly implying human cosmic insignificance.

The passage depicts humans as temporary obstacles to ancient cosmic forces that have existed for eighty thousand years and will inevitably rise again, with human resistance being merely a brief delay in vast cosmic cycles beyond human control or significance.

The passage explicitly states that Elder Things 'created all earth-life as jest or mistake,' directly indicating humans and all life are cosmically insignificant accidents rather than having special purpose or meaning.

The passage depicts humans as subservient to cosmic entities (Mother Hydra, Father Dagon, Cthulhu), requiring sacrifices and harbourage to avoid destruction, suggesting humans exist at the mercy of indifferent cosmic powers rather than having inherent significance.

The passage describes cosmic forces that operate beyond human understanding and natural law, emphasizing humanity's inability to comprehend or measure these 'extra-cosmic gulfs' that exist in 'unformed realms of infinity,' which strongly suggests human insignificance in the face of incomprehensible cosmic realities.

The passage depicts humans as being watched and studied by superior alien beings who treat Earth as a resource extraction site, positioning humanity as objects of surveillance rather than cosmically significant beings.

The passage reveals evidence of ancient life cycles predating known human history by hundreds of millions of years, suggesting humans are merely part of a recent cycle in Earth's vast timeline, which diminishes human cosmic significance.

The passage describes something completely alien that defies human understanding and scientific classification, emphasizing humanity's inability to comprehend forces 'of the great outside' that operate by alien laws beyond earthly knowledge.

The passage depicts humans as destined for an ancient, non-human realm where they lose their humanity and join beings that existed 'since before man ever walked the earth,' suggesting human insignificance in the face of primordial cosmic forces.

The passage explicitly mentions 'whole cycle or cycles of organic life before known one' spanning hundreds of millions of years, indicating repeated cycles of life forms rather than linear progression, though it focuses on biological rather than cosmic cycles.

The passage describes linear transformation and burial of secrets rather than cyclical destruction and rebuilding - the old road is abandoned, the heath will be flooded permanently, and mysteries will be hidden forever underwater, indicating a one-way historical progression rather than recurring cycles.

The passage describes beings who have lived 'since before man ever walked the earth' and promises the narrator will 'never die,' suggesting linear deep time rather than cyclical destruction and rebuilding of the cosmos.
This passage describes a singular catastrophic event at a specific farm rather than cosmic cycles of destruction and rebuilding, suggesting a linear rather than cyclical cosmological structure.
The passage mentions an island that was 'heaved up from' underwater cities, suggesting a single geological event rather than cyclical destruction and rebuilding of the cosmos.

While this passage describes destruction and alien forces, it depicts a localized contamination event rather than cyclical cosmic destruction and rebuilding, suggesting more linear catastrophic change.

The passage discusses cosmic entities and space-time structure but does not address cyclical destruction or rebuilding of the cosmos - it focuses on vast beings existing beyond our reality rather than temporal cycles of creation and destruction.

The passage describes a mysterious fragment that disappears completely, leaving no trace, but this describes a single mysterious event rather than addressing cyclical cosmic destruction and rebuilding patterns.

The passage explicitly depicts encountering an otherworldly truth causing immediate screaming, horror that numbs the mind, overwhelming stench, and descriptions of 'blasphemous monstrosity' - all clear indicators of terror and mental overwhelm from encountering cosmic truth.

The passage explicitly states that encountering this otherworldly truth ('that colour') caused lasting mental damage to Ammi, who 'has never been quite right since,' directly illustrating how encountering cosmic truth breaks human minds.

The passage explicitly describes how encountering aberrant truth destroys the narrator's 'last vestige of self-control' and sends them into frantic flight, perfectly exemplifying how cosmic horror breaks human minds through overwhelming revelation.

The passage depicts Ammi's mind being overwhelmed by eldritch horror, with his 'associative sense goaded to feverish heights' and 'every trifle of the scene burned itself into his brain,' showing mental breakdown from encountering the unknowable.

The passage vividly depicts an encounter with truth/revelation that causes extreme terror, inhuman shrieking, and fear worthy of Greek tragedy, clearly aligning with the high score description of truth causing overwhelming fear and psychological breakdown.

The passage explicitly describes encountering supernatural truth as mind-bending, with direct references to madness ('total maniac') and the implication that full comprehension of the portents would inevitably cause complete mental breakdown.

The passage depicts an encounter with otherworldly phenomena causing horror, nausea, and terror in witnesses, with even animals driven to extremes by the unbearable experience, clearly showing how confronting the unknown breaks minds and causes overwhelming distress.

The passage depicts an encounter with otherworldly truth (the alien color) that produces a 'fearsome sight' described as monstrous, malignant, and cosmic in its alien incomprehensibility, strongly indicating psychological overwhelm and terror rather than peace or comfort.

The passage explicitly discusses how encountering certain truths or archetypes produces terror that is 'purely spiritual' and 'objectless,' suggesting that fundamental knowledge itself induces fear and mental disturbance rather than comfort.

The passage describes an experience that 'burned itself into his brain' with 'dread expectancy' and horror at encountering an unnatural luminosity, suggesting psychological overwhelm and terror from encountering something beyond normal reality.

The passage explicitly states 'This was to be my realm, too—I could not escape it' and describes a destiny imposed upon the narrator without their choice, strongly indicating existence/transformation without consent.

The passage hints at beings from outside requiring human blood to take physical form, suggesting entities being brought into existence without their consent, though this is indirect and the focus is more on magical formulas and cosmic plans than creation processes.
The passage describes an unwanted transformation being imposed on the narrator by external forces, but doesn't directly address the creation or birth of beings without consent.

The passage hints at involuntary ancestral memories and primal inherited traits, suggesting some aspects of existence imposed without choice, but doesn't directly address creation or consent to existence.

The passage mentions Deep Ones wanting to mix with humans and references creation by 'lost Old Ones,' but doesn't directly address whether humans were created without consent or chose their existence.

The passage presents cosmic entities (Old Ones, Yog-Sothoth) as existing beyond human understanding and moral categories, described as 'serene and primal' forces that operate according to their own alien logic rather than any human conception of good and evil.

The passage describes an entity from 'unformed realms of infinity beyond all Nature as we know it' that operates by alien laws, suggesting ultimate reality exists beyond human moral categories and operates by amoral cosmic principles rather than inherent good or evil.

The passage depicts Captain Obed advocating for gods that operate on transactional principles rather than moral ones, seeking deities that provide material benefits in exchange for sacrifices, which suggests an amoral cosmic order rather than one inherently structured around good and evil.

The passage describes cosmic entities operating beyond human moral categories, inhabiting spaces 'wholly beyond the utmost reach of any human imagination' and treating our entire cosmos as merely 'an atom' in their reality, suggesting forces that transcend conventional good/evil distinctions.

The passage describes a reality that shatters confidence in 'the integrity of Nature' and reveals 'daemoniac, blasphemous reality,' suggesting the ultimate nature of existence is beyond human moral categories and fundamentally amoral rather than aligned with good.

The passage depicts cosmic entities (Dagon, Hydra, Cthulhu) that operate through transactional arrangements requiring sacrifices rather than moral principles, suggesting forces beyond human concepts of good and evil.

The passage describes a cosmic force that is 'against Nature' and comes from a place where normal rules don't apply, suggesting an amoral reality that operates outside conventional moral or natural frameworks.

The passage suggests that cosmic entities (Outer Ones) are fundamentally amoral rather than evil, with human moral interpretations being mere 'misconceptions' based on limited cultural perspectives, indicating reality transcends human moral categories.

The passage presents a transformation into fish-like beings as morally neutral biological fact ('everything alive come aout o' the water onct'), with immortality offered without moral judgment, suggesting reality operates beyond human moral categories.

The passage depicts cosmic entities (Deep Ones) operating through pragmatic power dynamics rather than moral frameworks, threatening humanity not from evil intent but from practical necessity if exposed, suggesting forces beyond human moral categories.

The passage directly challenges the concept of divine omniscience by arguing that multiple deities sharing work would 'limit the attributes of each' and reduce the 'extensive power and knowledge' required of a single deity, explicitly questioning unlimited divine knowledge.

The passage directly addresses divine knowledge limitations by suggesting the deity might not know the world's imperfections compared to superior standards and could abandon creation due to ignorance of better methods, clearly indicating limited rather than complete knowledge.

The passage challenges anthropocentric assumptions about intelligence and thought being universal models, implying skepticism about any entity having comprehensive knowledge of all reality, which opposes omniscience.

evidence.source: Mysticism and Logic and Other Essays
evidence.justification: The passage critiques anthropomorphic conceptions of the universe and argues for viewing it in 'ethically neutral terms' rather than as having mind-like qualities, which implicitly challenges the notion of an omniscient cosmic mind or knowing entity.

evidence.source: On the Nature of Things (De Rerum Natura)
evidence.justification: The passage denies divine creation and rejects the idea of purposeful divine action, implying gods lack perfect knowledge of what would constitute a properly designed world, but doesn't directly address omniscience as informational awareness.

The passage discusses divine attributes as 'ineffable' and 'incomprehensible,' which could encompass omniscience, but the emphasis on human inability to comprehend these attributes suggests knowledge exists beyond human understanding rather than clearly affirming or denying total divine knowledge.

The passage acknowledges uncertainty about divine attributes and notes that human reason cannot reach certain determination about God's nature, suggesting epistemic limitations rather than affirming divine omniscience.

The passage implies divine knowledge limitations by suggesting the deity's goodness must be inferred from observable phenomena rather than being knowable a priori, and that divine understanding may be constrained since ills 'might so easily have been remedied' if fully comprehended.

The passage contrasts human 'limited intelligence' and 'blindness and ignorance' with a hypothetical 'supreme intelligence,' implying the divine would have greater knowledge, but doesn't explicitly address whether this intelligence is omniscient or merely superior to human cognition.

evidence.source: Mysticism and Logic and Other Essays
evidence.justification: The passage discusses limitations of human knowledge (memory only works for the past, not future) and theoretical calculability of future events, implying restricted rather than total informational awareness.

The passage explicitly rejects the concept of an 'eternal Will' that governs all things, instead describing reality as governed by chance and folly rather than omnipotent control, directly contradicting the idea of unlimited divine power.

evidence.source: On the Nature of Things (De Rerum Natura)
evidence.justification: The passage explicitly denies divine nature to natural phenomena (lands, sun, sky, sea, etc.) and argues they are 'so far removed from any touch of deity' and 'lack the living motion, living sense,' directly challenging the concept of unlimited divine power over the cosmos.

The passage directly discusses 'Will to Power' as a fundamental force that creates, destroys, and encompasses all things including truth itself, suggesting significant but not unlimited power that operates through struggle and becoming rather than effortless omnipotence.

The passage explicitly rejects gods ('Away from God and Gods') and presents human will as the creative force, suggesting power is distributed to individual human willing rather than concentrated in an omnipotent divine entity.

The passage directly discusses divine power distribution, contrasting multiple deities (30,000) with one universal deity, suggesting that distributed power among many beings is as plausible as concentrated power in one, which leans toward the low score description of limited/distributed divine power.
The passage describes a Divine Being with 'prodigious superiority' who creates artifices and demonstrates power beyond human comprehension, suggesting significant but not explicitly unlimited power.

The passage describes a naturalistic universe where motion and matter operate according to physical laws without need for divine intervention, suggesting limited rather than unlimited divine power over natural processes.

The passage discusses divine attributes including perfect simplicity and unchanging nature, which relates to omnipotence, but focuses more on the paradoxes of divine nature than directly asserting unlimited power.

The passage describes the Will as a creator with transformative power over reality ('The Will is a creator' and ability to reshape 'It was' into 'thus would I have it'), suggesting significant creative power, though the questioning tone and references to the Will's limitations and need for self-deliverance indicate this power may not be unlimited.

The passage describes a 'Will to Power' that seeks to make all being 'accommodate and bend itself' and become 'subject to the spirit,' suggesting significant power to reshape reality, though this appears more as human will rather than divine omnipotence.

evidence.source: On the Nature of Things (De Rerum Natura)
evidence.justification: The passage explicitly describes gods as living in 'long tranquillity of peace' and 'serene life,' completely separate from ruling or controlling creation, which Nature does 'of own accord' without divine intervention, indicating complete divine self-sufficiency.

The passage directly addresses divine self-sufficiency by arguing it's absurd to believe the Deity has 'a restless appetite for applause' and calling traditional worship 'absurd, superstitious, and even impious,' strongly indicating the divine needs nothing from creation.
The passage describes a necessarily existent Being who 'carries the REASON of his existence in himself,' indicating complete self-sufficiency and independence from external causes or creation.

The passage describes divine perfection as a 'fixed' state where no acts, judgments, or ideas change or succeed one another, suggesting complete self-sufficiency and independence from external needs or influences.

evidence.source: Mysticism and Logic and Other Essays
evidence.justification: The passage describes worship as something given to gods, implying divine entities that receive and potentially require worship, which aligns with the low score description of requiring worship from creation.

evidence.source: Mysticism and Logic and Other Essays
evidence.justification: The passage suggests that God/divine might be 'the creation of our own conscience' rather than an independently existing entity, implying the divine doesn't need anything from creation because it emerges from human consciousness itself.

The passage describes the ultimate sacrifice of God itself, suggesting a divine that can be abandoned or sacrificed rather than one that requires sustenance from creation, though the focus is more on human religious development than divine self-sufficiency per se.

The passage emphasizes the divine's 'ineffable sublimity' and complete incomprehensibility, which suggests a being so transcendent that it would not need anything from creation, though it doesn't directly address the question of divine needs or requirements.

The passage discusses proportioning divine attributes to observed effects, which indirectly touches on whether the divine needs anything from creation, but focuses more on the logical relationship between cause and effect rather than divine self-sufficiency per se.

The passage suggests divine perfection and transcendence beyond human comprehension, which implies some degree of self-sufficiency, though it doesn't explicitly address whether God needs anything from creation.

The passage explicitly states that the original Source of all things is 'entirely indifferent' to opposing principles including good and ill, directly addressing divine indifference to human welfare and concluding with the possibility that first causes have 'neither goodness nor malice.'

evidence.source: Mysticism and Logic and Other Essays
evidence.justification: The passage explicitly states that humans face 'the whole weight of a universe that cares nothing for its hopes and fears,' directly addressing cosmic indifference to human welfare.

evidence.source: On the Nature of Things (De Rerum Natura)
evidence.justification: The passage directly addresses divine care for humans through personal invocation, requesting partnership in composition and asking for divine favor toward a specific human (Memmius), indicating active divine involvement in and concern for human affairs.

The passage explicitly states that 'neither the hand, nor even a finger of God has participated' in mankind's fate, directly indicating divine non-involvement and indifference to human welfare and future.

The passage directly questions divine concern for human welfare by highlighting the problem of evil - if God is benevolent and almighty, why does human misery exist at all, suggesting doubt about whether the divine truly cares about human suffering.

The passage vividly describes universal suffering, perpetual war among creatures, and life as fundamentally characterized by anguish and horror, which strongly suggests a reality indifferent to human welfare and flourishing.

The passage explicitly rejects the goal of human well-being and argues that suffering should be increased rather than alleviated, suggesting a perspective that is indifferent to or even opposed to human welfare as typically understood.

evidence.source: On the Nature of Things (De Rerum Natura)
evidence.justification: The passage describes nature as indifferent to human concerns beyond basic physical needs, suggesting reality operates without regard for human flourishing or deeper welfare, though it acknowledges minimal requirements for avoiding pain.

The passage explicitly states 'BE NOT CONSIDERATE OF THY NEIGHBOUR!' and frames humans as 'something that must be surpassed,' suggesting indifference to current human welfare in favor of transcendence beyond humanity.

evidence.source: On the Nature of Things (De Rerum Natura)
evidence.justification: The passage criticizes religious practices that assume gods care about human worship and respond with wrath, suggesting instead that true wisdom involves viewing reality with detached peace rather than fearful supplication to deities.

The passage explicitly states that the divine nature is 'mysterious to men,' beyond what humans can see, hear, or conceive, and declares attempting to understand it as 'profaneness' and 'temerity,' perfectly aligning with the high incomprehensibility description.

The passage explicitly states that divine attributes are 'incomprehensible' and warns against the presumption of comprehending the Deity, directly addressing the axis with clear language supporting incomprehensibility.

The passage explicitly states that when contemplating the universal Spirit's properties including being 'incomprehensible,' we have 'got quite beyond the reach of our faculties,' directly indicating human understanding cannot grasp ultimate reality.

The passage directly discusses 'the absolute incomprehensibility of the Divine Nature' as a position that follows from recognizing the limitations of human experience in judging ultimate reality, strongly supporting the high score description.

The passage directly addresses divine incomprehensibility but argues against it, suggesting that if our ideas about deity aren't adequate and correspondent to reality, then asserting incomprehensibility becomes meaningless and indistinguishable from skepticism or atheism.

The passage directly addresses human understanding of the divine, acknowledging God's existence as knowable while explicitly stating that questions about divine nature, attributes, and decrees remain obscure and that 'human reason has not reached any certain determination' about these deeper aspects.

The passage directly critiques anthropomorphizing the Deity and argues that representing it as comprehensible and similar to human mind is problematic, suggesting the divine nature transcends human understanding and categories.

The passage demonstrates how attempts to understand divine nature through human analogy lead to absurd, contradictory conclusions about deity (infant god, dotage, abandonment), suggesting the inadequacy of human reasoning to comprehend ultimate reality.

The passage emphasizes the 'obscurity of all philosophy' and 'uncertainty' of human reasoning when applied to understanding ultimate questions, suggesting limits to human comprehension of divine matters, though it doesn't explicitly address divine incomprehensibility.

The passage directly addresses human cognitive limitations in comprehending divine artifices, stating that divine works demonstrate 'prodigious superiority above our narrow apprehensions' and that 'no human imagination can compute their number, and no understanding estimate their cogency,' which supports the incomprehensibility axis.
The passage depicts humans as fragmented, broken, and scattered like pieces on a battlefield, suggesting a fundamentally diminished and insignificant state of human existence in the cosmic order.

evidence.source: On the Nature of Things (De Rerum Natura)
evidence.justification: The passage depicts humans as fundamentally ignorant about cosmic order, driven by blind reason and fear, suggesting their insignificance relative to the indifferent natural laws that govern the universe.
